audit_status.source_base: Hebrew Joshua / Yehoshua Rahab-and-spies narrative, draft aligned later
audit_status.review_note: First-pass draft reviewed for covert espionage, Rahab's occupation and agency, royal pursuit, concealed spies, Exodus/Sihon/Og memory, covenant oath, scarlet cord, household sanctuary, and return report.

verses.restored: Joshua son of Nun sent from Shittim two men as spies secretly, saying, Go, see the land and Jericho. They went and came to house of a woman, a prostitute/sex worker, whose name was Rahab, and they lay/stayed there.
It was told to king of Jericho, saying, Look, men from children of Israel came here tonight to search out the land.
King of Jericho sent to Rahab, saying, Bring out the men who came to you, who entered your house, for they came to search out all the land.
But the woman took the two men and hid them. She said, Yes, the men came to me, but I did not know from where they were.
It was at closing of gate, at darkness, that the men went out. I do not know where the men went. Pursue quickly after them, for you may overtake them.
But she had brought them up to roof and hidden them with stalks of flax arranged for her upon roof.
The men pursued after them by way of Jordan as far as fords, and gate was shut after pursuers went out.
Before they lay down, she came up to them upon roof.
She said to men, I know YHWH has given you the land, and that terror of you has fallen upon us, and all inhabitants of land melt before you.

For we heard how YHWH dried up waters of Sea of Reeds before you when you came out from Egypt, and what you did to two kings of Amorite across Jordan, to Sihon and Og, whom you devoted to destruction.
We heard, and our heart melted, and no spirit rose anymore in any man because of you; for YHWH your Divine, he is Divine in heavens above and upon earth beneath.
Now therefore, please swear to me by YHWH, because I have done covenant loyalty with you, that you also will do covenant loyalty with house of my father; give me a reliable sign,
that you will preserve alive my father, my mother, my brothers, my sisters, and all belonging to them, and rescue our souls/lives from death.

The men said to her, Our soul/life in place of yours for death, if you do not tell this matter of ours. It shall be, when YHWH gives us land, that we will do covenant loyalty and faithfulness with you.
She let them down by rope through window, for her house was in wall of city, and in wall she dwelt.
She said to them, Go toward hill country, lest pursuers meet you; hide there three days until pursuers return, and afterward go your way.
The men said to her, We shall be free from this oath of yours that you made us swear,
unless, when we enter land, you tie this cord of scarlet thread in window by which you let us down, and gather to yourself into house your father, mother, brothers, and all house of your father.

It shall be, anyone who goes out from doors of your house into street—his blood shall be upon his head, and we shall be innocent; but anyone with you in house—his blood shall be upon our head if a hand is against him.
But if you tell this matter of ours, then we shall be free from your oath that you made us swear.
She said, According to your words, so be it. She sent them away and they went, and she tied scarlet cord in window.
They went and came to hill country and stayed there three days until pursuers returned. Pursuers searched all way and did not find them.
The two men returned, descended from hill country, crossed over, and came to Joshua son of Nun; they recounted to him all that found them.
They said to Joshua, Surely YHWH has given all land into our hand, and also all inhabitants of land melt before us.

translation_notes: Rahab is named zonah, commonly 'prostitute' or 'sex worker.' The occupation should not be euphemized away or used to stigmatize her; the narrative presents her as politically perceptive, strategic, courageous, and capable of negotiating a binding covenant.
The spies' mission and Rahab's deception occur under threat from royal power. The chapter narrates protective lying without offering a simple universal rule about deception.
Rahab's confession of YHWH's sovereignty emerges from reports of liberation and conquest, including the destruction of Sihon and Og; her faith is therefore embedded in a climate of terror.
Her bargain is not only for herself but for an extended household, making the scarlet cord a sign of collective sanctuary.
The oath limits safety to those inside her house. This protection does not morally justify destruction of everyone outside it.
Rahab should not be reduced to a sexualized symbol or used in anti-sex-worker preaching. Her agency, intelligence, and covenant loyalty are central.
The narrative's conquest assumptions and language of inhabitants melting in fear must remain under anti-colonial and anti-terror safeguards.
safety_note: Do not use Joshua 2 to stigmatize sex workers, romanticize espionage, justify lying for domination, or imply that one family's rescue makes a city's destruction acceptable. Center Rahab's agency, courageous protection of vulnerable people, negotiated sanctuary, and the moral complexity of survival under approaching war.
